messages.content: I want to build a library management system
That sounds like a great project! Could you please tell me more about who the primary users of this library management system will be and what specific functionality they will need from the system?

It's for a university library with about 50,000 books and 5,000 students. The main users are students who borrow books, faculty members who need longer loan periods, and librarians who manage the entire system.

That's a good start. We have a clear understanding of who the users are. Now, let's dig into the specific functionality. Could you describe what key tasks or actions the students, faculty members, and librarians need to be able to perform in this system? For example, should students be able to search for books, reserve them, or maybe even renew them online?

Yes! Students should be able to search the catalog online, borrow books by showing their student ID, and see their borrowed books and due dates. Faculty can borrow more books for longer periods. Librarians need to add new books, track which books are borrowed, send overdue reminders, and generate reports on popular books.

That's very helpful information! Just to clarify the functionalities you've mentioned, I have a question about the librarians' tasks. When you say "generate reports on popular books", can you elaborate on what kind of data or insights you'd like these reports to provide? For instance, are we talking about the most borrowed books, books with the highest rating, or some other criteria?

Good question! The reports should show the most borrowed books in the last month and last year. Also, librarians need to see which books are currently overdue and who has them. We should track late fees too - $0.50 per day for regular books.

That's very insightful, thank you. I see that there's a late fee for overdue books. Can you clarify if this late fee applies to both students and faculty? Also, is there a maximum limit to these late fees or do they accumulate indefinitely until the book is returned?

Students pay late fees but faculty don't, since they have longer loan periods anyway. For students, the maximum late fee should be $20 per book - we don't want students avoiding the library because of huge fees! Also, students should be able to renew books online if no one else has reserved them.

Thank you for the clarification about the late fees for students and faculty. It makes sense to limit the maximum late fee to ensure the library remains accessible. You also mentioned the online book renewal feature for students if no one else has reserved the book. Could you elaborate on how many times a student can renew the same book online? And is there a certain period before the due date that they are allowed to renew it?

Students can renew books twice, so they can keep them for up to 6 weeks total (2 weeks initial + 2 renewals of 2 weeks each). They can renew anytime before the due date, but not after it's overdue. If someone reserves the book, the student gets an email notification that they can't renew and must return it.

Thank you for explaining the renewal process for students. It's clear that students can renew books twice, unless the book is reserved by someone else. Regarding the reservation process, could you please clarify how it works? For instance, how can users reserve a book? Is there a limit to the number of books a user can reserve at once? And what happens when a reserved book becomes available?

Users can reserve books through the online catalog by clicking "Reserve" if the book is currently checked out. Each user can reserve up to 3 books at a time. When a reserved book is returned, the system automatically sends an email to the first person in the reservation queue, and they have 48 hours to pick it up before it goes to the next person in line.

Thank you for explaining the reservation process in detail. It's very helpful to know that users can reserve up to 3 books at a time and they have 48 hours to pick up a returned book. I am curious about the reservation queue. Could you please clarify how it's managed? For instance, is there a limit to how many users can be in the queue for a single book? And what happens if a user doesn't pick up the reserved book within the 48 hours? Does it immediately become available for the next person in line or does it go back into general circulation?

There's no limit to the reservation queue size. If someone doesn't pick up within 48 hours, the system automatically moves to the next person in the queue and sends them an email. The book stays in the reservation system until everyone in the queue has had a chance, then it goes back to general circulation. That covers all the main requirements - do you have everything you need?

Thank you for the detailed explanation about the reservation queue system. That definitely helps me understand how it works. I would like to clarify a bit more about the notification system. Could you please elaborate on what kind of information the notification emails contain when a reserved book becomes available? Additionally, are there any other scenarios where the system sends notifications to the users, such as overdue reminders or upcoming due dates?
